name: Sigmund
description: Sigmund, a prominent figure in Norse mythology, is primarily known as a heroic mortal rather than a god, though his lineage connects him to Odin himself. He is a central character in the Völsunga saga, embodying traits of courage, strength, and tragic destiny. Sigmund is famed for his extraordinary prowess in battle and his legendary sword, Gram, which he drew from the tree Barnstokkr in the hall of King Völsung. His life is marked by both great triumphs and devastating losses, including the betrayal and death of his father and brothers. Sigmund's story is a testament to the themes of fate, heroism, and the cyclical nature of violence prevalent in Norse sagas. His relationship with his sister Signy, marked by incestuous union to produce the hero Sinfjötli, further complicates his character, highlighting the brutal realities and complex moral landscape of the Viking age. Ultimately, Sigmund's legacy lies in his role as the father of Sigurd (also known as Siegfried), a figure who would surpass even his own heroic achievements, solidifying Sigmund's place within the grand narrative of Germanic legend.
subtitle: Hero of the Völsungs

keyMyths.title: The Sword Gram
keyMyths.description: During the wedding feast of Sigmund's sister Signy, Odin appears disguised as a one-eyed old man and thrusts a sword into the Barnstokkr tree, declaring that only the one who can pull it out is worthy. Many try and fail, but Sigmund easily draws the sword, Gram, proving his inherent strength and divine favor. This act marks him as a chosen hero, setting the stage for his future exploits and ultimately leading to both glory and tragedy.

keyMyths.title: Sigmund and Signy
keyMyths.description: Sigmund and his sister Signy engage in an incestuous relationship, driven by Signy's desire to avenge her family's death. She believes that only a son of Sigmund can accomplish this task. Their union produces Sinfjötli, a powerful and fierce warrior. This act highlights the brutal and morally ambiguous world of the Norse sagas, where vengeance and survival often take precedence over conventional morality. The tale underscores the lengths to which characters will go to fulfill their obligations.

keyMyths.title: Sigmund's Death
keyMyths.description: Sigmund ultimately meets his demise in battle against King Lyngvi, one of the sons of Hunding, who had previously killed Sigmund's father. During the battle, Odin intervenes, shattering Sigmund's sword Gram. Fatally wounded, Sigmund is brought back to his hall, where he foretells the birth of his son Sigurd, who will be his avenger and a greater hero than himself. This scene emphasizes the themes of fate and cyclical violence inherent in Norse mythology.

sources.author: Byock, Jesse L.
